humanities_role_explanation: Includes humanities domains like History, Literature, Sociology, Psychology as subject areas within the multi-discipline benchmark, but not specifically designed for humanities analysis
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Multi-modal evaluation combining images with textual questions in both standard format (separate text and images) and vision-only format (questions embedded within screenshots/photos)
analytical_tasks_explanation: College-level questions requiring integration of visual and textual information across multiple disciplines, with emphasis on preventing shortcut exploitation and testing true multimodal understanding rather than simple factual recall

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Evaluates multimodal capabilities across six core disciplines: Art & Design, Business, Science, Health & Medicine, Humanities & Social Science, Tech & Engineering

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluated proprietary models (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 1.5 Pro) and open-source models (InternVL2, LLaVA-OneVision, VILA, MiniCPM-V, etc.)

llm_is_primary_system.note: Multimodal LLMs are the primary systems being benchmarked for their visual-textual reasoning capabilities

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes History, Literature, Sociology, Psychology within Humanities & Social Science discipline, plus Art Theory, Music within Art & Design discipline

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses accuracy metrics across different settings, with performance comparisons and statistical analysis

english_language.note: Paper written in English, though focuses on Arabic semantic understanding based on title and metadata

summary: MMMU-Pro introduces a more robust version of the MMMU benchmark that filters out text-only answerable questions, increases candidate options from 4 to 10, and adds a vision-only input setting where questions are embedded in images. The benchmark aims to prevent shortcut exploitation and test true multimodal understanding across college-level questions in six disciplines.
key_findings: All tested models showed significant performance drops on MMMU-Pro compared to original MMMU, ranging from 16.8% to 26.9% decrease. GPT-4o achieved 51.9% average performance (54.0% standard, 49.7% vision-only). Chain-of-thought prompting generally improved performance, while OCR prompts had minimal effect. The vision-only setting particularly challenged models' ability to integrate visual and textual information.
